text.title: Requirements Traceability — Core Concepts
text.summary: Traceability directions, coverage and gap analysis, link role vocabulary, and metrics with targets.

section.title: What Traceability Answers
section.p: Traceability links requirements to their origins and downstream artifacts. It answers four questions:

list.item: Where did this requirement come from? (upstream trace)
What does this requirement affect? (downstream trace)
Is every business need covered? (forward coverage)
Is every component justified? (backward coverage)

rule.statement: Maintain bidirectional traceability: forward from need to implementation (coverage) and backward from implementation to need (justification). Both directions are required for regulated audits.
rule.rationale: Forward coverage without backward traceability produces unjustified tests and code; backward traceability without forward coverage produces unverified requirements.

list.item: Forward — from business need to stakeholder req to solution req to design to code to test. Purpose: verify coverage.
Backward — from test to code to design to solution req to stakeholder req to business need. Purpose: justify every artifact.

rule.statement: Use exactly five link roles across the program: satisfies, derives, implements, verifies, conflicts. Reject any other role at lint time.
rule.rationale: A consistent role vocabulary enables automated coverage analysis; ad-hoc roles produce a matrix that no tool can query reliably.

list.item: satisfies — need → requirement (stakeholder need satisfies business need).
derives — requirement → requirement (solution req derived from stakeholder req).
implements — requirement → code or design artifact.
verifies — test → requirement.
conflicts — requirement to requirement (bidirectional conflict requiring resolution).

section.title: Traceability Strategy Levels
section.p: Choose the traceability depth appropriate to the project context:

list.item: Minimum — business req to functional req only. Appropriate for low-risk internal tools.
Standard — adds design to test links. Appropriate for most enterprise projects.
Full — adds code to user story to epic links. Required for regulated industries.

rule.statement: Pin coverage thresholds in CI: forward coverage at least 95%, backward coverage 100%, orphan count 0. Failure blocks the merge.
rule.rationale: Thresholds without enforcement are decoration; unchecked RTMs go stale within 2-3 sprints and give false assurance to auditors.

list.item: Are all business requirements traced to solution requirements? Gap = business requirement without solution requirement.
Are all solution requirements traced to tests? Gap = testing gap.
Are there orphan requirements with no parent? These require linking or deletion.
Are there tests without requirements? These are possible gold-plating or undocumented NFRs.
Forward coverage — (linked requirements / total requirements) x 100. Target: more than 95%.
Backward coverage — (justified items / total items) x 100. Target: 100%.
Test coverage — (requirements with tests / total requirements) x 100. Target: more than 90%.
Orphan count — items with no links. Target: 0.
